data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was primarily driven by a rapid and widespread shift in military technology and tactics, specifically the adoption of massed, mobile infantry (javelin-throwers and swordsmen) that rendered the elite, chariot-based armies of the palatial states obsolete.", "mechanism": "The palatial states' power and internal legitimacy rested on a military monopoly held by a chariot-riding aristocracy. The development of new infantry tactics and weaponry (like the Naue II sword and javelin) by peripheral or 'barbarian' groups allowed them to overwhelm the expensive and tactically rigid chariot forces. This military defeat was swift and decisive, leading to the sacking of palaces and the collapse of the administrative centers that controlled the economy. The 'Sea Peoples' were a manifestation of this military revolution, not its cause.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows the widespread destruction was caused by a prolonged siege or a weapon system effective against fortifications, rather than by assault troops overwhelming defenders in the field.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Textual evidence shows the palatial chariot armies successfully adapted to and defeated the new infantry tactics in multiple major engagements.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its New Kingdom military, under Ramesses III, had already begun incorporating Sherden and other mercenaries familiar with these infantry tactics, allowing them to adapt and counter the threat at the Battle of the Delta, unlike the more conservative Hittite and Mycenaean forces."}
